topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The hammering started before the sun was fully up, a relentless, grating sound that scraped against my nerves. I stood by my window, my jaw tight, watching the neighbor’s crew wrestle with the posts. Every swing of the hammer felt like an assault on my peace. I could feel a hot flush rising in my neck, a tight knot coiling in my stomach. They were so loud, so intrusive, as if the very air was being torn apart by their construction. I wanted to scream, to tell them to stop, but the words caught in my throat, replaced by a cold, hard resentment that settled deep in my chest. I just stared, my knuckles white where I gripped the windowsill, wishing the whole irritating spectacle would vanish into thin air.

[story 2]

"Are you going to finish this today?" I asked, my voice clipped and sharp, the edge honed to a razor's point. I watched from across the lawn as the man with the bright orange vest carelessly stacked lumber. His casualness grated on me; it was a complete disregard for the quiet order I desperately tried to maintain. My shoulders were hunched, a defensive posture taking over, and I noticed the tremor in my hands as I leaned against the porch railing. Every movement felt deliberate, fueled by a simmering irritation that had been building for weeks. I didn't want to engage, but the sheer presence of their activity made my skin crawl, a prickly, unwelcome sensation that demanded distance.

[story 3]

I spent the entire afternoon pacing my front yard, my boots crunching on the gravel, a restless energy vibrating off me. The sight of the new fence posts, stark and imposing against the green, only intensified the sour feeling churning inside me. I kept running a hand over my face, feeling the tension radiate from my scalp down to my fingertips. When the neighbor finally called out, asking if I had any objections, I managed a tone of forced calm, a thin veneer over the deep, churning dissatisfaction. "It's fine," I managed, the sound tasting like ash in my mouth. I didn't look at them; my gaze was fixed on a distant, empty patch of sky, wishing I could simply evaporate.
